lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Synthesis and close
Let me bring this home.
We've covered a lot of molecules in two hours, and if you try to hold them in your head as a flat list of names, you'll lose them by next week.
So don't.
Instead, hold the architecture, and let the individual drugs hang off it.
Here's the architecture in one breath.
Every antibiotic works by exploiting a difference between a bacterial cell and your cells, and there are five such differences.
The cell wall — attacked by beta-lactams, which inactivate the cross-linking transpeptidase, and by vancomycin, which caps the D-Ala-D-Ala substrate; biggest difference, safest drugs.

The ribosome — attacked at the 30S by aminoglycosides, which cause lethal misreading, and tetracyclines, which block tRNA docking, and at the 50S by macrolides, clindamycin, chloramphenicol, and linezolid, which jam various steps of elongation and initiation; toxicity tracking the resemblance to your mitochondrial ribosomes.

The nucleic acid machinery — fluoroquinolones trapping DNA gyrase mid-cut and shredding the chromosome, rifampin blocking RNA polymerase, metronidazole activated only inside anaerobes to fragment their DNA.
Folate synthesis — sulfonamides and trimethoprim hitting two sequential steps of a pathway you don't even possess, synergizing into a bactericidal combination.
And the membrane — daptomycin and the polymyxins tearing it open; smallest difference, most toxic, last resort.
And wrapped around all of it: four resistance strategies — destroy the drug, change the target, keep it out, pump it out — each one predictable from the drug's own mechanism.
If you remember nothing else, remember the two organizing principles, because they're the ones that turn this from memorization into understanding.
First: the size of the selective-toxicity gap predicts the safety of the drug.
When the bacterial target has no human counterpart — the cell wall, the folate pathway — the drugs are beautifully safe.
When the target faintly echoes something in our own cells — the ribosome shadowed by our mitochondria, or the membrane we share in lipid form — the toxicity creeps in.
You can almost read a drug's side-effect profile straight off its mechanism.
Second: the mechanism of action predicts the mechanism of resistance.
Tell me precisely how a drug binds its target, and I can tell you how bacteria will most likely evade it — by altering that target, by destroying the drug, by refusing it entry, or by ejecting it.
Those two principles are the difference between a list and a framework.
The list you'll forget.

The framework will let you place a drug you've never seen before — and you will meet new ones, because we keep inventing them in the arms race — and reason out roughly how it must work, how toxic it's likely to be, and how it'll eventually be defeated.
That arms race is the real reason this matters beyond the exam.
Every mechanism we covered today is, simultaneously, a target the bacteria are working to neutralize.
The drugs that felt invincible a generation ago are failing now, which is exactly why a toxic relic like colistin is back in our hands.
Understanding these mechanisms isn't academic housekeeping — it's the foundation for using these drugs wisely enough to keep them working, and for designing the next generation when these ones fall.
The bacteria are very, very good at evolution.
Our advantage is that we can understand — and understanding the mechanism is where it all starts.
That's where we'll stop.
Next session we'll take these mechanisms into the clinic and talk about choosing among them for specific infections.
Thanks, everyone — good work today.
